claims.claim: Speculative decoding accelerates autoregressive inference by drafting token sequences with a cheap model and verifying them against a target.

claims.claim: In a controlled synthetic benchmark with a 12-layer deterministic autoregressive target, we train multinomial linear heads at early layers to imitate the full target's greedy next-token decisions and simulate speculative decoding under an explicit verifier-cost model.

claims.claim: Speculative decoding reduces the latency of autoregressive generation by proposing multiple candidate tokens in parallel and verifying them against a target model in a single forward pass.

claims.claim: We construct a synthetic 12-layer autoregressive target with accessible internal states, train auxiliary linear heads at selected early layers to mimic the full target's greedy decisions, and compare against the naive strategy of reusing the final LM head on early states.

claims.claim: Auxiliary heads work conditionally.** A trained linear head at layer 3 achieves 86.6% accuracy against the full target's greedy labels and produces drafts with 76.5% per-token acceptance, yielding an estimated 1.33× speedup.

claims.claim: The naive approach fails decisively.** Applying the final LM head to layer-3 hidden states achieves only 1.4% accuracy—far below the 44.0% majority-class baseline—and produces no accepted drafts.

claims.claim: Deeper exits trade cost for accuracy.** A layer-2 exit in preliminary runs showed higher acceptance (~86%) but the calibration and final runs converged on layer 3 as the best speedup point, as deeper exits consume more of the target's compute budget.

evidence_refs.quote: Speculative decoding accelerates autoregressive inference by drafting token sequences with a cheap model and verifying them against a target. We investigate whether a shallow prefix of the target model itself—augmented with a lightweight auxiliary exit head—can serve as the drafter, eliminating the need for a separate draft model.
